## Introduction

Propositional logic is the study of the inferential relations that obtain
between compound propositions, such as:

$$
\textrm{Alice is a philosophy major \emph{and} Bob is majoring in economics.}
$$

$$
\textrm{\emph{Either} the train is late \emph{or} the schedule that is posted online is wrong}.
$$

$$
\textrm{\emph{If} the battery is not dead, \emph{then} the computer will turn on.}
$$

$$
\textrm{You are eligible to vote \emph{if and only if} you are a resident of the county}.
$$

Propositional logic forms the core of most other systems of logic utilized in
philosophical analysis, including first-order quantificational logic and modal
logic. It also provides the standard context in which the theory of
probability is developed. It thus constitutes the first essential entry in a
philosophy student's logical toolkit. In this chapter, we will introduce a
formal language in which to represent arguments of propositional logic, a
semantic framework in which to assess their validity, and a proof system in
which to carry out propositional reasoning.
